context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m stuck on this 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class, and idk how to connect conflict, development interventions, and China in one answer. can u help me figure out what the prompt is really asking?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Good starting point — breaking down the prompt is exactly the right move. So, read the prompt again carefully. How many distinct questions is it actually asking you to address? Can you list them in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and needs orientation toward the prompt. Rather than explaining the prompt for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to break it down themselves. I'll ask them to identify the distinct sub-questions in the prompt, which is the natural first step before writing anything.

exchanges.student: uh maybe like 2 or 3? tbh it all kinda blends together for me rn.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough that it feels blurry — but let's not skip this step, it'll make your writing much easier. Go back and re-read the prompt one more time, then try to list what you see as the separate questions. Even a rough attempt is fine. What do you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being vague. I should push them to actually attempt to list the questions rather than accepting 'maybe 2 or 3.' This is a straightforward task they can do if they re-read the prompt carefully. I'll be direct and ask them to try listing them explicitly.

exchanges.student: ok rough try: 1) how development stuff affects conflict before/during/after, 2) whether China changes the usual development-security model, and maybe 3) whether security + development should be merged more.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's exactly right, three distinct questions. Now here's the important thing: a reflection piece isn't just answering each one separately. Do you see any connection between those three questions? What thread might link them together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the three sub-questions. I should affirm this and then push them to think about how these three questions relate to each other, since the reflection needs to be coherent rather than three disconnected answers. This moves them toward planning their argument.

exchanges.student: idk maybe they’re all about whether development is actually helping or making things messier? that might be off tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a promising instinct — don't dismiss it. What would make development interventions 'messier' in a conflict context specifically? Can you think of a concrete example or mechanism where a development intervention might backfire or complicate things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on an interesting track but being tentative. I should push them to develop this intuition further rather than validating or dismissing it outright. The connection they're sensing is legitimate — there's a real tension in the literature about whether development interventions can inadvertently fuel conflict or whether security and development are mutually reinforcing. I want them to think more concretely about this.

exchanges.student: maybe aid goes to one group more than another and then ppl see it as biased? ngl I keep guessing tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a well-known dynamic in the literature, not just a guess. So if aid distribution can fuel grievances, what does that tell you about the relationship between development interventions and conflict drivers — are they separate things, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a real and important mechanism — unequal distribution of aid fueling grievances. I should affirm this as a legitimate idea without giving more away, then push them to connect this insight to the broader argument structure of their paper. I want to move them forward rather than dwelling on examples.

exchanges.student: maybe they’re not separate at all? like interventions can become part of the conflict drivers, but I’m still kinda lost big picture tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight and a solid foundation for your argument. Now, how might that idea connect to the China question? Does China's approach to development change or challenge the dynamic you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a key insight — that development interventions can themselves become conflict drivers. This is a substantive point. Rather than elaborating for them, I should push them to now connect this insight to the other two sub-questions (China, and security-development convergence). This moves them toward seeing the full argument arc of their reflection.

exchanges.student: maybe bc China does more infrastructure/no-strings stuff? can u just explain the whole big picture version bc I’m not seeing how it all fits.
exchanges.tutor: I won't lay it out for you — that's your job as the writer! But look at what you already have: interventions can become conflict drivers, and China's approach looks different. What does 'no strings attached' imply about how China sees the relationship between security and development? Does it converge them or separate them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the big picture for them, which would violate the core rule of never providing the answer directly. However, they have made real progress and have good instincts. I should redirect them back to building the argument themselves using what they've already figured out. The student has identified: (1) interventions can fuel conflict, (2) China takes a different approach. I should push them to think about what the 'no strings attached' approach implies for the security-development nexus.

exchanges.student: tbh I can argue either way rn, which is why I’m stuck. that angle didn’t fully work, so maybe I need a simpler frame.
exchanges.tutor: Being able to argue both ways isn't a problem — it means you're thinking critically. But a reflection piece requires you to take a position. Which way do you actually lean, based on what you know? Pick one and explain why, even tentatively.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling stuck because they can argue both ways, which is actually a sign of good critical thinking rather than a problem. I should point this out and push them to recognize that taking a position is part of the assignment — a reflection piece requires them to commit to an argument. I should also remind them they have enough material now to start drafting, rather than continuing to search for a 'perfect' frame before writing.

exchanges.student: I lean that too much convergence is bad bc it turns development into control, but I still need the overall structure fr.
exchanges.tutor: That's a clear, defensible position — good. You actually already have three building blocks from our conversation. Try sketching a rough outline using them. What would you put first, second, third to support that argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now committed to a position, which is great progress. They're asking for overall structure, but rather than giving it to them, I should push them to build it themselves using what they've already developed in our conversation. They have: a central argument (too much convergence turns development into control), an insight about interventions becoming conflict drivers, and a point about China's different approach. I should ask them to try to sketch a structure using these elements.

exchanges.student: can u give me just a super general outline shape? I’m blanking rn.
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that — the structure needs to come from you. But you're not actually blanking: you have a central argument, a mechanism that supports it, and a China angle that complicates or reinforces it. That's already three pieces. What order would make logical sense to a reader?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide an outline, which would be giving them part of the solution. I should refuse but redirect them productively. They already have all the pieces from our conversation — I should remind them of that and push them to assemble it themselves. I'll be firm but encouraging.
